# Chapter 22

[j4t8n2c7] Flynn drove like the road was a fuse and he was chasing the flame. The Charger had a new noise since Diesel went up in the air, a thin metallic whine that came and went with throttle, but he treated it like a voice you ignore when you’re busy bleeding. The storm was behind him now. Ahead the desert opened into a hard brightness where the heat lay like glass over the asphalt. Wrecks marked the line the way bones mark a trail. A coupe on its roof with its wheels still spinning lazily. A truck nose-down in a wash, steam drifting from a split radiator, the driver sitting in the shade of the wreck with his helmet off and his hands in his lap like he was waiting for permission to die. Flynn did not slow. He felt the car skate over patched sections of highway, the suspension compress and rebound, and he held it straight with fingers that had stopped shaking because they had become something else.

[p0x6m9a1] The radio had been mostly static since the mountains, everyone talking over everyone else, men swearing in Spanish, women laughing like it was a party, then long stretches of nothing where you could hear only engine noise and breathing. Now voices came through cleaner as the terrain flattened and the air stopped eating signal. Mateo’s calm drawl from somewhere ahead. A checkpoint official barking numbers. Someone crying that their oil pressure was gone. Cage’s laugh cut through once, bright and sharp, and then vanished. Flynn clicked his mic and asked where the Shelby was. A pause, as if the people listening had to decide whether to answer. Then a voice he didn’t know said, He’s second. He’s hunting. Like it was an animal report on a nature feed. Flynn looked at the dash overlay and saw the little icons, jittering, the system trying to make clean sense of chaos. He didn’t trust it, but he used it anyway.

[6w2k7d0q] He passed a Camaro with its hood strapped down by rope, the driver’s head moving in tiny jerks as if each bump was a punch. The Charger’s speed climbed and climbed. One eighty. One ninety when the pavement was clean. The engine screamed like a living thing caught in a trap, and the sound filled Flynn’s skull until his thoughts were just pressure and heat. The desert around him was a blur of scrub and rocks and occasional solar arrays like black ponds. He remembered Diesel’s weight in his arms, the way her blood had soaked through his shirt, and he used the memory the way Cage used laughter. Fuel. The world had gone narrow. There was the line of road and the far point where it disappeared into shimmer and nothing else. His implant kept offering warnings in the corner of his vision. Stability risk. Engine temp. He blinked them away until the only message left was distance: 100 miles.

[b8c1r5v9] The highway turned into an older highway, patched and narrowed, with old concrete barriers that had been cracked by heat and repaired with scrap steel. The Charger’s tires hummed on the smoother surface, a different kind of danger because speed on clean ground felt safe until it wasn’t. Flynn saw the remains of a town, just foundations and windblown streets, a dead billboard still advertising a water park that no longer existed. On a side road a pod-transport station sat abandoned, its charging docks sand-choked, a relic of the safer world watching the outlaw road with empty eyes. The absurdity of it hit him. Somewhere inside the cities, people sat in cooled rooms and watched a live feed of this race like it was history reenacted, something to be moved by without consequence. He wondered if Khoury was watching from a hospital bed. He wondered if Diesel’s monitor was still pinging green as the helicopter carried her away. He wondered if the system would bring her back just enough to suffer.

[t3n7h0s4] A cluster of cars ran ahead of him, three abreast, fighting for lanes like children fighting over a toy. Flynn came up on them so fast his stomach lifted. He didn’t horn. He didn’t flash. He slid left into the oncoming lane where the faded yellow line meant nothing, and he held it there as the wind tried to peel the Charger off the pavement. Faces turned in those other cars, helmets and goggles catching sun. One driver swerved at him as if to say no, you don’t get this. Flynn kept coming. At the last second the man blinked and yielded, because the only argument out here was mass and commitment. Flynn passed them one by one, close enough to see stickers and bullet holes, close enough to smell hot brakes. When he was clear he cut back in and the road opened again, empty, and he felt the strange calm of having no one left to negotiate with.

[4q9f2j6u] The chatter shifted. A voice said Mateo’s out front by a mile. Another said the Boss Mustang was still whole, still singing. A third said the Shelby was closing on him and laughed like it was good news. Flynn listened and felt the numbers settle into place. Mateo would win. Cage didn’t care. Cage had never cared about winning as a thing you held up for proof. Cage cared about the moment when you could have died and didn’t. Or when you could have lived and chose not to. Flynn pressed the throttle until the pedal was hard against the floor. The Charger answered with violence. The hood trembled. A seam of heat shimmered off the fenders. His hands were steady now, but his mouth tasted like pennies, the body’s old alarm system firing even when the mind told it to shut up. Fifty miles. The road began to climb gently, not mountain, just a rise that lifted him into a different air where he could see farther.

[z1m5c8p3] He saw the Shelby as a white slash on the horizon, two stripes like scars, the car’s shape too perfect for this landscape. It was a museum piece made feral. Behind it the air shook with exhaust and heat. Flynn felt something in him loosen, a knot he’d been carrying since the compound. Fear, yes, but also a kind of relief. The monster was real, and he had a body, and bodies could be stopped. He gained in inches, then yards, then car lengths. The Shelby stayed in the center of the lane like it owned the road. Flynn could see Cage’s head through the rear glass, the posture relaxed, one hand high on the wheel like he was cruising. On the passenger side there was a smaller silhouette, Birdie’s, rigid and low. Flynn remembered the kid’s face when Diesel told him to go. The boy had nodded like he understood, and maybe he had, and maybe he’d still climbed into Cage’s car anyway because worship is stronger than comprehension.

[k7v0d4x2] The last of the other racers dropped away. They’d wrecked or backed off or fallen into their own private battles. Now it was just two cars and a stretch of asphalt that ran like a blade through desert. Flynn drew up until he was at Cage’s quarter panel and the noise between them became a physical thing, engines screaming in parallel, wind hammering the windows. He looked right. Cage’s face turned toward him and the grin was immediate, as if he’d been waiting for this the whole time. His eyes were bright, pupils small in the glare, and the grin wasn’t friendly, it was hungry. Cage shouted something, and Flynn couldn’t hear it, but he could read it on his mouth. Isn’t this everything. Cage spread his fingers on the wheel like a showman, like the road was a stage he’d built just to prove a point. Flynn looked past him to Birdie. The kid’s head snapped toward Flynn and the terror in his eyes was clean and adult and sudden. He was not a believer in that moment. He was a passenger in a coffin going two hundred.

[1s8l3a9g] Flynn felt the choice like a weight settling into his hands. Back off and live. Let Cage take the finish line and the next line and the next, let him keep burning through people until the desert had eaten them all. Survive and carry Diesel’s blood on his shirt as a souvenir. The implant in Flynn’s neck tried to produce a rational menu, options and probabilities, and he could feel it working like a small nervous animal under his skin. But the desert didn’t do menus. Diesel’s voice came back, not what she’d said but the look, the fury that was also grief. Wrench’s broken face in the med tent. Khoury’s hands tied, his pleading. Yuki’s car falling into the void. Flynn understood then that Cage wasn’t a man with bad ideas. Cage was a principle. Nothing mattered, therefore everything was permitted. Flynn took a breath, tasted hot rubber, and moved the Charger a few inches right until his front fender was aligned with the Shelby’s rear quarter.

[n6e2u7y0] The first impact was small, almost polite. Metal kissed metal and screamed. The Charger shuddered and the steering wheel kicked in Flynn’s hands. Cage snapped his car back left in reflex and the Shelby’s tires chirped as rubber fought for grip. Flynn held steady. He felt the whole world tilt into that contact point, as if all the speed and all the distance were being converted into one narrow argument of force. Cage looked over again, grin still on his face, and then the grin thinned. He said something else, a curse or a laugh, and his mouth moved fast, excited. Flynn didn’t answer. He nudged again, harder. Sparks flew in a bright spray, the kind you see at night when someone grinds steel, but here they were daylight sparks, brief and furious, and the sound was like tearing cloth made of tin. Birdie’s mouth opened in a silent scream. His hands were up on the dash like he could brace himself against physics.

[c0p4w9r1] Cage fought back. He shoved the Shelby into the Charger’s door and the impact boomed through Flynn’s ribs. The Charger slid half a lane and the tires sang, and for a second Flynn saw the shoulder rushing up, the dirt that meant spin, flip, death. He corrected by instinct and felt the car catch. Cage’s laughter returned, not happy now, but sharp with insult, as if Flynn had broken a rule of the game. Cage wanted danger, but only on his terms. Flynn saw it in his eyes: surprise at being challenged, anger at being treated like any other man. They ran side by side at a speed that made the road markings turn into a continuous band. Heat rose off the hoods and made both cars look like they were underwater. In the distance a thin smear of green showed where chilli fields began, the finish region, the place Don Castellanos had made a garden in hell. Mateo was out there somewhere, a dot going about the business of winning. Flynn’s business was smaller and darker.

[5h1b7k2t] Flynn hit him again. He timed it to a slight bend where the road leaned, where weight transferred. The Charger’s fender bit into the Shelby’s quarter and the Shelby twitched. Cage’s head snapped toward him, and for the first time Flynn saw something like calculation flicker there, the quick inventory of risk. Cage had never expected to die today because Cage never expected consequences. But physics didn’t care about belief. The Shelby’s rear stepped out a foot. Cage corrected, too much throttle, tires breaking loose on old asphalt dusted with sand. The Shelby wobbled. Cage’s grin was gone now. His mouth formed a hard line. He was working. Flynn felt a strange pity for him, not because Cage deserved it but because Flynn could see the shape of Cage’s fear and it was childish. Cage’s whole philosophy depended on the idea that death was reversible. The moment the car began to slide, that philosophy turned into a lie he could taste.

[r9d3q6m8] Birdie moved. It was sudden, a blur of skinny arms across the cabin. He reached for the wheel from the passenger seat, not to help Cage but to take it, and Flynn saw the decision on his face like a light switching on. He wasn’t saving Cage. He was saving himself. He was saving whatever was left of him that hadn’t been burned out by worship. Birdie yanked the wheel left, away from Flynn, away from the Charger’s fender. The Shelby jerked. Cage’s hands fought, but Birdie had leverage and desperation. For a half second the Shelby cleared, and Flynn thought it might straighten and run, might escape with its wounds. Then the overcorrection took. The tires caught the edge of a seam in the pavement and the car snapped into a yaw that was too fast to catch. The Shelby’s nose pointed at the sky for an instant, then toward the shoulder, then back toward Flynn, rotating on its own axis like a thrown knife.

[u2x8n0c5] Time slowed the way it slows when you know you’re watching something you can’t stop. Flynn’s foot came off the gas without him meaning to. The Charger surged forward anyway on momentum. The Shelby was beside him and then it wasn’t; it was a spinning white shape, stripes turning into circles, the world reflected in its glass in fragments. Flynn saw Cage’s face through the window as the car rotated. Rage, yes, but also a quick flash of understanding, the moment the brain admits what the mouth has denied. The shoulder was dirt and gravel. The Shelby hit it with its rear tires and the dirt exploded in a brown fountain. Traction vanished. The car continued to rotate, faster now, the spin tightening. The sound changed from engine scream to the sickening hiss of rubber skimming over loose grit. Birdie’s head was thrown back. His mouth was still open. Flynn saw his hands on the wheel, white-knuckled, and then saw them slip.

[a7g5l1v4] The Shelby left the pavement. It went over the edge like a dancer stepping off a stage. The shoulder dropped into a shallow ditch lined with rocks and old drainage culverts. The car’s front end dug in and the whole body pitched forward. The first flip was clean, almost graceful, the roof coming down and the tires pointing at sky. The second flip tore metal loose. The hood peeled back like a lid. Glass erupted in a glittering spray that caught sunlight and turned it into a brief rain of knives. Flynn’s Charger roared past, his eyes locked on the wreck as if watching could change the outcome. He felt the urge to brake, to stop, to run, but his body kept the wheel straight because at this speed stopping was its own death. He kept pace with the catastrophe for a heartbeat. The Shelby rolled again, slower, heavy, the desert reaching up to grab it. The sound was not loud so much as deep, the earth being hit by something that had believed it could outrun the earth.

[9m0t6s2p] The road ahead was empty. The finish was somewhere beyond the green smear of fields, beyond the next rise, beyond the place where men would be cheering and cameras would be waiting and Don Castellanos would be smiling like a priest. Flynn didn’t know yet what he would do when he got there. He only knew what he had done. In his mirror the dust plume from the Shelby’s roll rose like a signal fire. The radio crackled with sudden frantic voices, questions layered over questions, someone screaming a name that might have been Birdie’s. Flynn didn’t answer. He drove in the silence between breaths, the Charger still fast, still hot, still alive. He thought of Diesel in the helicopter, eyes open, telling him to stop him. He thought of Cage’s grin fading into something like a child being told no. And he thought of the desert, patient as ever, taking what was offered. Behind him metal was still tumbling. Ahead the line of road ran on. He kept it centered.
